text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 58-year-old male with known Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutant, microsatellite stable), status post laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy and currently on maintenance 5-fluorouracil/bevacizumab following dose reduction and discontinuation of oxaliplatin due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy. Routine restaging examination. Prior imaging available for comparison (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, cycle 10 restaging, approximately 4.5 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL) administered via peripheral IV at 3 mL/sec. Acquisitions in portal venous phase (70-second delay) for abdomen and pelvis, and in venous phase for chest. Axial slices reconstructed at 3 mm with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats provided. No oral contrast was administered per protocol. Tube current modulated; radiation dose CTDI vol 14.2 mGy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. There is no endobronchial lesion. The lungs are otherwise well-aerated without consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based masses.

Of note, two pulmonary nodules are identified that were definitively absent on all prior imaging:

  -- Right lower lobe: There is a new, well-circumscribed, round, solid pulmonary nodule measuring approximately 8 mm in greatest axial dimension, located within the posterior basal segment (series 3, image 187). The nodule is subpleural in location with smooth margins. There is no internal calcification, cavitation, or surrounding ground-glass halo. No satellite nodules are identified in the ipsilateral lower lobe.

  -- Right upper lobe: A second new solid pulmonary nodule is identified in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe, measuring approximately 5 mm (series 3, image 112). This nodule is also smoothly marginated without calcification.

Both nodules are new compared to the most recent prior CT (cycle 10 restaging) and were absent on all previous examinations including the staging CT obtained at diagnosis. In the appropriate clinical context of a patient with known metastatic colorectal carcinoma and rising serum tumor markers, these findings are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastatic disease. A primary lung malignancy or infectious/granulomatous etiology is considered less likely given their bilateral laterality, smooth morphology, and the overall clinical picture, though cannot be entirely excluded on imaging alone.

No additional pulmonary nodules are identified in the left lung.

Pleurae:
No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or pleural-based nodularity.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The mediastinal and hilar soft tissues do not demonstrate pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. No mediastinal mass. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. The pulmonary artery is not enlarged. The esophagus is unremarkable.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic bony lesions in the visualized ribs, clavicles, scapulae, sternum, or thoracic vertebrae. No acute fracture. Mild multilevel degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is of normal morphology and contour. The previously described hepatic metastases are again identified within segments II, IVb, VI, and VII. There has been subtle interval growth in the larger lesions compared to the prior cycle 10 restaging CT, consistent with loss of disease control:

  -- Segment VI: The largest hepatic lesion measures approximately 1.8 cm in axial diameter (previously 1.5 cm on prior comparison, +20% in size). It remains a hypoattenuating, relatively well-defined lesion on portal venous phase imaging. There is no internal hemorrhage or capsular retraction. Rim enhancement pattern is similar to prior.

  -- Segment IVb: The lesion in segment IVb now measures approximately 1.3 cm (previously 1.0 cm, +30% in size). The lesion is hypodense relative to the surrounding hepatic parenchyma and is not centrally necrotic.

  -- Segment II: The left lobe lesion in segment II now measures approximately 0.7 cm (previously 0.5 cm, slight interval increase). It remains a small, rounded hypoattenuating focus.

  -- Segment VII: The segment VII lesion measures approximately 0.5 cm (previously 0.4 cm, roughly stable within measurement variability).

The margins of all lesions remain distinct without evidence of biliary duct invasion or satellite nodularity. There is no new hepatic lesion identified. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. No perihepatic ascites.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without calculi, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct measures 4 mm and is not dilated.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is visualized in its entirety and is unremarkable. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size, measuring approximately 11 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesion. No perisplenic fluid.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are of normal size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size with smooth cortical contours. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. No solid renal mass. There is a 6 mm simple-appearing right renal cyst, unchanged from prior. The ureters are not dilated bilaterally.

Bowel:
The colon demonstrates post-surgical changes consistent with prior sigmoid colectomy and primary anastomosis. The anastomotic region appears intact without perianastomotic soft tissue thickening, abscess, or fistula formation. No wall thickening of the visualized colon or small bowel. No bowel obstruction. No free air. No bowel wall mass.

Sigmoid Colectomy Site:
No evidence of local recurrence at the resection site. The presigmoid/presacral soft tissues are within normal limits with no soft tissue mass or abnormal lymphadenopathy.

Peritoneum and Mesentery:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental caking to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The mesentery is unremarkable.

Abdominal and Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes:
No pathologically enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or celiac lymph nodes by size criteria. Several sub-centimeter lymph nodes are noted in the portocaval and para-aortic regions, unchanged from prior.

Abdominal Aorta and Great Vessels:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysm. The celiac, SMA, and SMA branches are patent. The IVC is unremarkable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PELVIS:

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is adequately distended and unremarkable in appearance. No wall thickening, calculus, or intraluminal filling defect.

Prostate:
The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with age-appropriate benign prostatic hypertrophy. No nodule or mass. No seminal vesicle abnormality.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No enlarged iliac, obturator, or inguinal lymph nodes identified.

Bones and Soft Tissues (Pelvis):
No lytic or sclerotic lesion in the visualized pelvic bones, iliac wings, sacrum, or proximal femora to suggest osseous metastatic disease. No pelvic free fluid.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. NEW bilateral pulmonary nodules, highly suspicious for metastatic disease in the clinical context of known KRAS-mutant metastatic colorectal carcinoma with rising serum CEA:
   -- New right lower lobe posterior basal segment solid nodule, 8 mm.
   -- New right upper lobe posterior segment solid nodule, 5 mm.
   These nodules were not present on prior imaging and are the most clinically significant new finding on this examination.

2. Hepatic metastases (segments II, IVb, VI, VII): Subtle but measurable interval growth of the larger lesions compared to the cycle 10 restaging CT, with the dominant segment VI lesion now 1.8 cm (previously 1.5 cm) and segment IVb 1.3 cm (previously 1.0 cm). The segment II and VII lesions show slight increase as well. These findings suggest that disease control has been lost on the current maintenance regimen. No new hepatic lesions.

3. No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis, pleural metastases, osseous metastases, or pathologic lymphadenopathy.

4. Anastomotic/resection site remains clear without locoregional recurrence.

Overall, the combination of new pulmonary nodules and slight growth of existing hepatic metastases in the setting of concurrently rising tumor markers (CEA 41 ng/mL, up from prior 22 ng/mL) is consistent with radiographic disease progression on maintenance systemic therapy. Clinical correlation and oncology discussion recommended.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist Signature]
Dictated and Electronically Signed
